description: The Kagyed Dance at Rumtek Monastery is a major year-end masked-dance ritual symbolizing the destruction of negative forces and the welcoming of harmony, peace, and prosperity for the upcoming year. Performed by monks in elaborate masks and robes, the dance includes ritual movements, traditional musical instruments, and the ceremonial burning of effigies to represent purification of the old.
highlights: Powerful Cham masked-dance performances by monks
Ritual burning of effigies to dispel negativity
Spiritual atmosphere marking the end of the Tibetan year

endDate: 2nd or 3rd day of the 1st Tibetan Month
description: Losar at Rumtek Monastery marks the Tibetan New Year and begins with prayers, purification rituals, and traditional offerings to welcome harmony and good fortune. Monks perform special ceremonies, scripture recitations, and butter lamp rituals, creating a deeply festive and spiritual atmosphere.
Sacred masked-dance ceremony reenacting episodes from the life of Guru Padmasambhava. Symbolizes the triumph of wisdom over negativity with elaborate masks, costumes, and monastic music.

startDate: Full Moon of the 4th Tibetan Month
description: Saga Dawa commemorates the birth, enlightenment, and parinirvana of Lord Buddha. Monks perform special prayers, butter-lamp offerings, and merit-making rituals; devotees circumambulate and offer prayers.
A large-scale chanting ceremony where monks recite thousands to millions of mantras for world peace and healing, creating a deeply meditative spiritual atmosphere.
A profound tantric ritual practice invoking Vajrakilaya for protection and purification. Includes extensive mantra recitations, mandala offerings, and sacred dances.
Part of year-end purification rituals before Losar. Monks perform powerful protector practices dedicated to Mahakala with sacred dances, fire pujas, and ritual chants.

startDate: 18th day of the 12th Tibetan Lunar Month
endDate: 19th day of the 12th Tibetan Lunar Month
description: Detor Cham is the most important public festival of Enchey Monastery. Monks perform sacred masked dances that represent protector deities and symbolic cosmic figures, believed to dispel negative forces and purify the environment. The festival draws large crowds who witness elaborate masks, costumes and traditional ritual music.
highlights: Costumes representing deities and mythic beings
Traditional music with horns, drums & cymbals

time: Morning prayers, afternoon rituals, cultural programs
description: Pang Lhabsol honors Mt. Khangchendzonga, the protector deity of Sikkim. The festival symbolizes the historic brotherhood pact between Bhutia and Lepcha communities, expressing unity and cultural harmony. Monks perform ritual dances representing guardian deities, accompanied by chanting and ceremonial offerings.
highlights: Community gathering with deep cultural significance

description: Singhe Chaam is a rare sacred masked-dance ritual performed once every three years. Monks enact symbolic dances representing protective deities and mythological beings to symbolize the victory of good over evil and spiritual protection.

startDate: 1st day of the 1st Tibetan Lunar Month / Sikkimese Losoong
description: Losar and Losoong mark Tibetan & Sikkimese New Year celebrations at Enchey Monastery, including sacred prayers, purification ceremonies, masked Cham dances, and butter-lamp offerings for blessings and prosperity.

Daily and seasonal pujas include mantra chanting, scripture recitations, butter-lamp offerings and protective rituals performed by resident monks to maintain spiritual balance and bless the community.

startDate: 14th–15th day of the 1st Tibetan Lunar Month
time: Morning – vase opening ceremony, Afternoon – holy water distribution
description: Bumchu is the most important festival at Tashiding Monastery where a sacred vase of holy water is opened once a year. The water level is interpreted as a prophecy for the coming year, predicting peace, prosperity or difficulties. Monks perform ritual prayers and devotees receive holy water blessings.
highlights: Yearly prophecy based on water level

description: Pang Lhabsol honors Mount Kanchenjunga, protector deity of Sikkim, and celebrates the historic bond between Bhutia and Lepcha communities. Monks perform ritual dances and prayers invoking protection and blessings.

startDate: 1st day of the 1st Tibetan Lunar Month (usually February–March; varies yearly)
time: Morning to evening prayers, rituals, and offerings
location: Dubdi Monastery, Yuksom, West Sikkim, India
description: Losar at Dubdi Monastery is the most celebrated festival known from public sources. As one of the oldest monasteries in Sikkim, Dubdi observes Losar with ritual prayers, ceremonial offerings, and monastic chanting to usher in a new year filled with peace, prosperity, and spiritual merit. Devotees visit to circumambulate the monastery, light butter lamps, and participate in blessings from the resident monks. Though public documentation is limited, Losar is considered a period of spiritual renewal, community gathering, and preservation of centuries-old Buddhist traditions at Dubdi. The festival blends devotion, ritual, and cultural identity, highlighting the monastery’s historical significance as a center of Nyingma Buddhism.
highlights: Ritual prayers and chanting led by resident monks
Lighting of butter lamps and offerings for spiritual merit
Circumambulation of the monastery by devotees
Community participation reinforcing spiritual and social bonds
Observance of the Tibetan lunar calendar traditions
Spiritual significance: protection, prosperity, and purification
Celebration of one of the oldest functioning monasteries in Sikkim
Preservation of Nyingma Buddhist heritage and cultural identity

startDate: 28th–29th day of the 10th Tibetan Lunar Month (approximately November–December)
location: Dubdi Monastery, Yuksom, West Sikkim, India
description: Dubdi Monastery, as per historical and heritage records, observes year-end rituals corresponding to the 28th–29th day of the 10th Tibetan month — similar in spirit to the Kagyed festivals of other Sikkimese monasteries. While public sources do not give detailed programs, these rituals traditionally involve monks performing symbolic offerings, prayers, and protective ceremonies to cleanse the monastery of negative energies and invoke blessings for the coming year. The event fosters spiritual renewal, moral reflection, and communal participation, emphasizing Dubdi’s heritage as one of the oldest centers of Nyingma Buddhist practice in Sikkim.

Symbolic purification of monastery grounds and community spaces
Spiritual blessings for protection, peace, and prosperity

location: Sanga Choeling Monastery, Pelling, West Sikkim, India
description: As one of the oldest monasteries in Sikkim (est. 17th century), Sanga Choeling Monastery serves as an active pilgrimage site and cultural heritage center. Devotees and tourists visit to perform worship, meditation, and offer prayers at ancient statues, murals, and clay idols. Pilgrimage and heritage observances often coincide with other Buddhist festivals, but are also ongoing throughout the year. These visits promote spiritual reflection, cultural appreciation, and continuity of the Nyingma monastic tradition.
highlights: Worship and meditation at historical statues, clay idols, and murals
Continuity of Nyingma lineage spiritual practices
Cultural immersion in one of Sikkim’s oldest monasteries
Year-round opportunity for devotion and pilgrimage

description: Gonjang Monastery, a Nyingma‑lineage gompa near Gangtok, reportedly holds a Cham (masked‑dance) festival during the 10th Tibetan month. Monks don traditional masks and robes and perform ritual dances that dramatize Buddhist mythological stories — aiming to cleanse negative energies, express spiritual truths, and bless devotees and the wider community. The festival offers a rare opportunity for visitors and pilgrims to witness living Himalayan Buddhist culture in a serene setting, with the monastery’s peaceful ambience, prayer‑hall art, and panoramic views adding to the spiritual atmosphere.
